title: OSI Model
summary: The OSI model divides network communication into 7 layers so engineers can isolate and troubleshoot issues quickly.
diagram: L7 Application -> L6 Presentation -> L5 Session -> L4 Transport -> L3 Network -> L2 Data Link -> L1 Physical
example: If a web page does not load, check DNS and HTTP (L7), TCP handshake (L4), and IP reachability (L3).
troubleshooting: Start from L1 and move upward: cable/link lights, interface status, VLAN assignment, IP addressing, routing, then application service status.

title: TCP/IP
summary: TCP/IP is the practical networking model used on real networks, combining multiple OSI layers.
example: A TCP session to port 443 uses a 3-way handshake and reliable delivery.
troubleshooting: Verify transport port and protocol, then verify IP path and MTU if packets are fragmented.

title: IPv4 Addressing
summary: IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses with network and host portions defined by subnet mask.
example: A /24 mask gives 256 addresses, 254 usable host addresses.
troubleshooting: Check duplicate IPs, wrong subnet masks, and default gateway mismatch.

title: Subnetting
summary: Subnetting creates smaller networks for efficient addressing and broadcast control.
example: 192.168.1.0/26 creates 4 subnets, each with 62 usable hosts.
troubleshooting: Confirm network and broadcast addresses are not assigned to hosts and masks match across peers.

title: VLAN
summary: VLANs segment Layer 2 domains and reduce broadcast scope.
example: PCs on same switch but different VLANs need Layer 3 routing to communicate.
troubleshooting: Check access VLAN assignment, trunk allowed VLAN list, and native VLAN consistency.

title: STP
summary: Spanning Tree Protocol prevents Layer 2 loops by blocking redundant paths.
diagram: Root bridge elected -> Root ports + designated ports forwarding, non-designated blocked
example: Adjust bridge priority so core switch becomes root for deterministic traffic flow.
troubleshooting: Watch for topology changes, unexpected root bridge, and portfast misuse causing loops.

title: Routing Fundamentals
summary: Routers forward packets between networks using longest prefix match and routing tables.
diagram: LAN A -> R1 -> WAN -> R2 -> LAN B
example: A static route 0.0.0.0/0 sends unknown traffic to an upstream gateway.
troubleshooting: Verify next-hop reachability and check route administrative distance conflicts.

title: OSPF
summary: OSPF is a link-state protocol that uses areas and SPF to build optimal paths.
diagram: Area 0 backbone with ABR connecting Area 10
example: Two routers form adjacency when hello/dead timers and network type match.
troubleshooting: Check area mismatch, passive interfaces, and MTU mismatch for stuck EXSTART/EXCHANGE states.

title: ACL
summary: Access Control Lists filter traffic based on source, destination, protocol, and ports.
diagram: Inbound ACL on WAN interface blocks unauthorized traffic before entering LAN
example: Permit 10.10.10.0/24 to web server, deny other sources.
troubleshooting: ACLs are processed top-down; verify order and implicit deny at bottom.

title: NAT
summary: NAT translates private IP addresses to public addresses for internet access.
example: PAT allows many internal hosts to share one public IP using unique ports.
cli: ip nat inside source list 1 interface g0/0 overload
troubleshooting: Ensure interfaces are marked inside/outside and ACL includes correct internal networks.

title: DHCP
summary: DHCP automates IP assignment with leases and options.
example: A branch router can serve as local DHCP server for clients.
troubleshooting: Check excluded-address range, helper-address on relay interface, and pool network mask.

title: DNS
summary: DNS resolves hostnames to IP addresses using hierarchical records and caching.
example: nslookup confirms whether resolution fails at client or DNS server.
troubleshooting: Validate server reachability, correct records, and TTL cache behavior.

title: Wireless Networking
summary: Wireless networks use SSIDs, channels, and security policies to deliver mobility.
diagram: Client -> Access Point -> WLC -> Distribution switch
example: Use non-overlapping channels 1, 6, 11 in 2.4 GHz to reduce co-channel interference.
troubleshooting: Check RF interference, authentication failures, and weak signal at edge coverage zones.

title: Network Security Basics
summary: Baseline security includes segmentation, least privilege, secure management, and monitoring.
example: Disable unused switch ports and place them in an unused VLAN.
troubleshooting: Audit open management services and verify AAA fallback to prevent lockouts.
